Coral bleaching

Coral bleaching is the process by which corals lose their characteristic colour and become white, typically as a stress response to changes in environmental conditions such as elevated sea-surface temperature, low salinity, or pollutants. The white appearance results from the expulsion or death of zooxanthellae, the symbiotic algae that live in the coral's tissues and provide most of its energy through photosynthesis.

Mechanism

Reef-building corals form an obligate mutualism with single-celled algae of the genus Symbiodinium. Under normal conditions these algae photosynthesise inside the coral tissue and transfer up to 90 percent of the resulting energy to the host. When water temperature rises by 1–2 °C above the long-term local maximum, the symbiosis becomes destabilised: the algae overproduce reactive oxygen species, the coral host begins to digest or expel the algal cells, and the underlying white aragonite skeleton becomes visible through the now-transparent tissue.

If the stress is short-lived the algae can recolonise the coral and colour returns. Bleaching events lasting more than a few weeks typically result in coral mortality, with downstream consequences for the thousands of reef-associated species that depend on the structure that living corals provide.

Major bleaching events

The first globally synchronous mass bleaching event was recorded in 1998, coinciding with a strong El Niño. Subsequent global-scale events occurred in 2010, 2015–2017, and 2023–2024. The 2023–2024 event was the most severe ever recorded, affecting an estimated 84 percent of the world's reef area, including widespread bleaching across the Great Barrier Reef, the Mesoamerican Reef, and reefs throughout the Indo-Pacific.

Recovery and resilience

Recovery depends on the duration of thermal stress, the local nutrient regime, and the resilience of individual coral species. Branching corals (Acropora, Pocillopora) bleach earliest and recover slowest. Massive corals (Porites, Diploria) are more thermally tolerant. Active interventions under investigation include selective breeding of heat-tolerant strains, microbiome modification, and probiotics.
